text:
Rechargeable zinc (Zn) metal batteries are severely limited by aqueous corrosion, as solvated zinc ions continuously transport active water molecules to the electrode surface. In this work, we introduce a "sacrificial solvation shell" strategy using methanol as an electrolyte additive to displace water from the Zn²⁺ coordination sphere. This approach facilitates the in situ formation of a fluoride-rich, organic-inorganic gradient solid electrolyte interphase (SEI) layer. This unique interphase—comprised of an organic-rich exterior and an inorganic-rich (ZnF₂) interior—synergistically provides high mechanical flexibility and rapid ion diffusion paths while physically shielding the anode from water-induced degradation.

The methanol-modified electrolyte enables exceptionally stable and corrosion-free zinc plating/stripping. On copper substrates, we demonstrate over 1,000 cycles with an average Coulombic efficiency of 99.95%. In symmetrical Zn/Zn cells, the system achieves a record-high cumulative plating capacity of 10 Ah/cm² at an aggressive current density of 40 mA/cm². Furthermore, we evaluated the practical potential of this chemistry in VO₂//Zn full cells. Using an ultralow N/P ratio of 2 and a thin 20-μm Zn foil, the battery maintained a high areal capacity of 4.7 mAh/cm² for over 250 cycles. By achieving commercial-level capacities with thin zinc anodes, this work provides a simplified manufacturing route and a robust strategy for developing long-life, grid-scale aqueous energy storage devices.
